content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Why a single ‘warning’ severity let AI-generated slop reach readers.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I opened the dashboard expecting another routine PR review and found a lie — a post with the forbidden guilt phrase ‘I’m not proud of that.’ Why did that post go live? Because my pipeline’s ai_slop gate was set to warning mode.
Warnings don’t block. They log and move on. That post reached readers before I spotted it, and that pushed me to act.
The Warning That Rarely Blocked
My pipeline had a gate called ai_slop_detector. It scanned drafts for forbidden phrases, emotional performance, and reader CTA bait.
When it found a violation, it set ai_slop_passed=False. In gate_aggregation.py at line 92 (per commit d4bb7d3), that finding was classified with severity="warning".
Warnings are polite. They tell you something is off, then let you proceed. My pipeline treated them as advisory.
Per my pipeline’s routing logic, any post with a quality score of 0.92 or higher triggered a fast-path that skipped each gate. I configured that fast-path in route_after_quality: if the score was high enough, the draft bypassed each subsequent gates.
